slides.title: The Typedef Shortcut ✂️
slides.content:
Typing `struct Player myPlayer;` over and over gets annoying.

C provides the **`typedef`** keyword to create shortcut aliases for data types.

slides.title: Using Typedef
slides.content: By wrapping our struct in a `typedef`, we tell C: "Rename this entire block to just `Player`."

quizData.question: What is the primary benefit of using `typedef` with a struct?

options.text: It makes the struct run faster.
It removes the need to type the word `struct` every time you declare a variable of that type.
It protects the variables from being deleted.

quizData.explanation: `typedef` is purely syntactic sugar. It saves keystrokes and makes code cleaner.

slides.title: The Shape-Shifter: Union 🎭
slides.content:
A **Union** uses the exact same syntax as a Struct, but has one insane difference:

**All members inside a Union share the exact same physical memory address.**

If a union has an `int` and a `float`, you can ONLY use one of them at a time, because writing to the `int` will literally overwrite the memory of the `float`!

slides.title: Union Overwriting
slides.content: Watch how assigning a string completely destroys the integer that was stored at the same memory location.

quizData.question: If you have a struct with 3 ints, it consumes 12 bytes. If you have a union with 3 ints, how much memory does it consume?
quizData.explanation: A union only reserves enough memory for its single largest component, because every member is strictly stacked on top of each other at the exact same location.
